persons.arguments_against: The counter-case would be two C. M. Lushingtons in the Ceylon service, or one stint belonging to a relative. It fails because the surname is rare (only 5 officials and a SINGLE biography for it), and both stints form one continuous Ceylon career whose offices map to the bio by name and place. Stint 1 (1877-1900): Writer (bio 'writer, Ceylon civ. serv., 1st Apr., 1876'), Police Magistrate (bio 'pol. mag.... 1877'/'magistrate, 1878'), Office Assistant, and Assistant Government Agent of the Eastern Province by 1900 (bio 'govt. agt., E. prov.... 13th June, 1903'). Stint 2 (1907-12): Collector / Government Agent of the Southern Province, matching the bio exactly: 'govt. agt., S. prov., 7th Dec., 1905; govt. agt. and fiscal for S. prov., 22nd Sept., 1907'. The position_sim FAIL on stint 2 is a phrasing artifact ('Government Agent' vs 'govt. agt., prov. of Uva'); the printed Southern-Province agency matches the bio. With a single bio for a rare surname and an unbroken matching career, a namesake is not credible.

evidence_cited.fact: C. M. Lushington | Assistant Government Agent | Eastern Province
govt. agt. and fiscal for S. prov., 22nd Sept., 1907

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second C. M. Lushington in the Ceylon service -- which the single biography for this rare surname makes implausible.

notes: Rare surname, single bio. Two stints are the early (1877-1900) and late (1907-12) segments of one continuous Ceylon career; the Eastern-Province and Southern-Province agencies match the bio by name.
